title: Electronic correlations driving Chirality-Induced Spin Selectivity
abstract: We explicitly account for electron-electron interactions when modeling low-dimensional helical organic molecules. We show that competition between various hopping channels, together with interaction-induced double- and superexchange mechanisms, can stabilize non-collinear helical magnetic order. The resulting single-electron bands exhibit partial spin polarization, a manifestation of $p$-wave magnetism. Using density-matrix renormalization group, cluster perturbation theory, and Monte Carlo methods, we find that even vanishingly small spin-orbit coupling triggers strong spin selectivity at temperatures significantly above the spin-orbit scale. While strong correlations are essential for this mechanism, long-range spin ordering is not required. We thus propose non-collinear spin correlations driven by Coulomb interactions as an explanation of chirality-induced spin selectivity and discuss connections to experiments.
summary: This paper theoretically explains Chirality-Induced Spin Selectivity (CISS) in helical organic molecules by modeling electron-electron interactions. It demonstrates that strong, non-collinear spin correlations, rather than relying solely on spin-orbit coupling or long-range magnetic order, are the primary drivers of spin polarization. The research utilizes advanced numerical methods like DMRG and CPT to validate this mechanism.
main_problem: The paper addresses Chirality-Induced Spin Selectivity (CISS)—the spin polarization of currents through chiral media—by investigating the role of electron-electron interactions.
main_result: Non-collinear spin correlations driven by Coulomb interactions are proposed as the mechanism for CISS, showing that strong correlations, rather than long-range magnetic order or large spin-orbit coupling, are sufficient to induce spin selectivity.
method: The study employs advanced theoretical techniques including Density-Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG), Cluster Perturbation Theory (CPT), and Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) simulations.
model_or_system: The model focuses on low-dimensional helical organic molecules, utilizing an extended two-orbital model incorporating on-site Coulomb ($U$) and Hund's-rule ($J_H$) interactions, leading to an effective Kondo-lattice model.
key_observables: Spin polarization in transmitted electrons, one-particle spectral functions ($N(\omega)$ and $N(k, \omega)$), and the spin-structure factor $S(\vec{k})$.
important_parameters: The mechanism is shown to be robust even when the spin-orbit coupling (SOC) is vanishingly small, provided strong correlations are present.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Kondo-lattice model derived from considering the orbital-selective Mott phase transition, and CPT is noted to only include short-range correlations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate DMRG results showing spin-polarized density-of-states and momentum-resolved spectral density, and classical spin simulations showing spin-structure factors peaking at incommensurate wavevectors.
paper_structure: The paper develops the model by incorporating interactions into a two-orbital framework, applies various numerical methods (DMRG, CPT, MCMC) to calculate spectral properties, and concludes by proposing the role of non-collinear spin correlations in explaining CISS.
why_interesting: The work provides a detailed theoretical framework for understanding spin-selective transport in molecular systems, linking correlation physics (Hubbard/Kondo models) directly to a key phenomenon in spintronics and molecular electronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Cartoon of the two-orbital model motivated by Ref. [21]: each site contains ’radial’ orbital pr and ’tangen- tial’ pt, see top left. The spiral is shown from the side (top) and from above (bottom left). The spin-spin angle in the correlation-driven spin spiral (bottom right) is set by corre- lations, while SOC only selects the spin plane (orthogonal to the spiral axis) and the winding direction.

FIG. 3. Cluster perturbation theory results based on short-range correlations. Color indicates spin polarization. Panel (a) is based on an eight-site KLM (1) and (2) with λ = 0.015 tt, Jt = 0.48 tt and l = 1, remaining parameters as in Fig. 2. Similar as in Fig. 2(b), the solid lines in represent the effective band splitting ωeff(k) = 2teff cos(k) ± 2λeff sin(k), with teff ≃0.45 and λeff ≃0.1. Panel (b) depicts results for a twelve-site Hubbard model combining (3) and SOC (2) with λ = 0.01|t1| and l = 2. Parameters: t1 = −1, U = 8 |t1|, t2 = 0.3 |t1|, and n = 2/3 filling.

FIG. 2. Density matrix renormalization group method re- sults for the KLM (1) with l = 1 Rashba SOC (2). (a) Fre- quency dependent density-of-states N σ(ω) without and with Rashba SOC (2). For the latter, both spin projections are presented. (b) Momentum k resolved one-particle spectral density N(k, ω) for both spins, red and blue represent ↑ and ↓projections of ⃗d · ⃗σ, respectively. (c) Spectral weight N(k, ω = µ) at Fermi level µ. Solid lines in (b) represent the band splitting by the effective (interaction-induced) SOC cou- pling ωeff(k) = 2teff cos(k) ± 2λeff sin(k), with teff ≃0.4 and λeff ≃0.25. Note that λeff is an order of magnitude larger than the one used in a full calculation....

FIG. 4. Markov-chain Monte-Carlo results for the classi- cal spin KLM. (a) Spin-structure factor S(k) for in-plane (x, y) and out-of-plane (z) components of spin and various temperatures. Parameters: NN and NNN hoping tt = −1, tt,2 = 0.25 |tt|, respectively, NN and NNN Heisenberg spin exchange Jr = 0.8 |tt|, Jr,2 = 0.5 |tt|, respectively, coupling to localized spins 2JH|Si,r| = 2 |tt|, n = 0.8 filling, and SOC λ = 0.02 |tt|, l = 2. (b) Density of states N σ(ω) for left-moving states for parameters as in (a). (c) Momentum k resolved one-particle spectral density A(⃗k, ω) for σ =↑at β |tt| = 80, remaining parameters as in (a).
